title: Samsara - INFINITY27
shortDescription: Freelance gameplay systems shipped for Samsara, a live UE5 title developed through INFINITY27.
fullDescription:
Samsara is a live UE5 game for which I contributed gameplay systems as a freelance developer, following completion of the INFINITY27 UE5 skills bootcamp. 

I built two systems following a real production workflow: a stacking attack speed passive in Blueprint with VFX feedback scaling to stack count, and a modular health regeneration Actor Component in C++ supporting flat and stat-scaled regen via a timer-based approach.

Both were designed with exposed variables for designer tuning and integrated cleanly into the existing codebase. I also diagnosed and fixed live bugs including multi-hit collision causing incorrect stack accumulation and a flat-vs-percentage scaling error in attack speed. Work continued post-delivery, extending both systems into boss drop behaviour and refactoring Blueprint graphs for maintainability.

title: Duality Shift
shortDescription: A third-person puzzle platformer built in UE5 with real-time world switching via Data Layers.
fullDescription:
A third-person puzzle platformer built in Unreal Engine 5, centred on a real-time world-swap mechanic that uses UE5 Data Layers to toggle between two distinct versions of the same level.

I designed and implemented the core system, managing object visibility, collision, and interaction states across both worlds to ensure consistent, predictable behaviour on every swap. Puzzle design was driven by forced mechanic usage: pressure plates requiring cross-world object placement, platforms hidden between worlds, and hazards existing in only one state.

Equal weight was given to player readability, structuring layouts so safe and unsafe states are immediately legible and swap outcomes feel logical rather than trial and error. The project demonstrates the full loop from technical implementation to level design iteration, with the system and the puzzles built to reinforce each other.

title: Signal Lost [In Development]
shortDescription: A VR escape room built in Unity 6 with a gestural multi-interaction system designed to keep players immersed and comfortable throughout.
fullDescription:
Signal Lost is a VR escape room currently in development, built around a multi-interaction system where players can grab objects directly or flip their hand to switch into a ray-cast mode. The gestural mode switch means players are never broken out to a button or menu, and the force-pull specifically solves a core VR immersion problem: objects are never frustratingly out of reach due to real-world physical constraints.

Information lives on physical objects, and audio and lighting function as mechanics rather than atmosphere. Puzzles are mechanically distinct and solvable in any order with no handholding. Players observe, experiment, and figure it out.

The project demonstrates end-to-end ownership of a coherent VR system, a strong instinct for player feel, and the ability to design interactions that keep players present, comfortable, and engaged throughout.
